title: The Naming Conventions in Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: The Naming Conventions in Evangelion             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Tue Jun 23, 2015 1:00 pm For the intents and purposes of this inquiry, I understand fully how Christianity/Judaism are strange/mystical/esoteric and considered "occult" in Japan. What I am curious about is the "in-universe" causality of why Geherin/Nerv/Seele started naming these "giant alien beings" after angels or why Naoko named the Magi "the Magi" instead of "Naoko 5000" (or something to that effect). Now, I get that the naming convention was something on Anno's part as far as the window-drssing thematic goes, and perhaps it was even his meta commentary on how arbitrary such conventions tend to be when generally applied to more Eastern aesthetics/spiritual conceits in other anime, possibly even poking fun at how creatures/beings (which are clearly named after one found in mythology) simply are named what they are because "that's their name" (with no further elaboration). However, that still doesn't explain why otherwise super-rational and exceptionally intelligent scientists would start giving nominal placeholders to their giant cloned alien bodies disguised as robots. Now, I can sort of understand the application of the word "God." In that regard, it's a representation of a powerful force/intelligence that simply exists high above and behind the scope of what even our most powerful tools/science can analyze/decipher. However, naming the giant alien creatures that attack after Biblical/Hebrew messengers, doesn't make sense in a country where said religion (which is proven to be a real thing in the Eva-verse via Shinji's conversation with Asuka in ep. 11 about "why they are fighting angels"). So basically, my question is, what is the in-universe explanation for why they name things the way do in regards to bi things like the Seeds/Angels/Magi/Evas? I'm of the mind to think that the lack of explanation seems to be part of why the initial reaction from the West(which I understand was NOT the intended audience for various reasons including religion) place such an emphasis on the "spiritual" aspects of the series during the early years of the fandom. In a sense, this question also relates to the "Dead Sea Scrolls," which we know are basically "Alien Instructions" but they are conflated with the "real ones' by everyone just calling them that...of which are they supposed to be same ones found in the 1940s and this just Anno's way of playing off that mystery? If so, it's extraordinary that a religion would coincidently (within Evangelion itself) align itself almost so perfectly to events that otherwise have nothing to do with religion. In other words, how is it that the "names of the Angels" found in the DSS's are also found in the Talmud/Torah, etc. which are clearly proven to exist in the Eva-verse, DESPITE literally having no other connection to the story or events and CERTAINLY having no connection whatsoever to the Judaic-Christian God(s) Jesus/Yahweh/Holy Spirit?

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 23, 2015 1:17 pm Probably because Seele are a bunch of religious fanatics. Other than that, who knows?

[]:             Quote Post by Ispellnogood » Tue Jun 23, 2015 1:18 pm It sounded good to them. There really isn't too much meaning. Kind of like how you see other animes reference other deities and shit.

[]: Re: The Naming Conventions in Evangelion             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Jun 23, 2015 1:19 pm SawItAtAge10 wrote: why Naoko named the Magi "the Magi" instead of "Naoko 5000" (or something to that effect).

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 23, 2015 1:53 pm Yeah. Also the implication is that the various names for the angels came from the FAR, and the desert folk who first found them built a religion around misunderstanding what the text was saying.

[]:             Quote Post by Falcon_of_the_Sun » Tue Jun 23, 2015 2:02 pm All the Judeo-Christian imagery is just window dressing, but I love it. I think it's hard to imagine a unified belief or willingness or taste from different characters in deciding to go for certain biblical namings. I mean, I can't see much of a reason as to why the namings and whatnot decided by Seele (i.e. 7-eyed mask on Lilin, the name Lilin itself if you want etc), by Gendo (the painting on his office ceiling), Naoko (naming the computer after the Three Wise Men) plus other random things (Asuka's mentioning of Gericho, the pillars of salt in post-SI Antarctica etc) and so on can be pinned down to some sort of common knowledge. Consequently, to me it's a clear case of the authors deliberately creating some sort of fil rouge to hint at a possible spiritual, divine perspective for the whole story. Whereas the more traditional sci-fi reading of NGE would be that of the evolution of a species in the Darwinian sense (humanity vs angels), I like how the spiritual perspective (humans and angels trying to obtain some kind of divine status) is a good intermediate layer betwee the evolutionary one and the psychological one. Let's not forget that this "path to divinity" is basically the main concept behind Japanese shintoism, which in turn is something present in many a manga/anime (pretty much most sport ones). For the record, the Dead Sea Scrolls found in Qumran (I mean, the real ones) are important because, once dated, they truend out to be one of the oldest "copies" of parts of what is considered canon Old Testament for Jewish people.

[]:             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Tue Jun 23, 2015 2:54 pm Falcon_of_the_Sun wrote: All the Judeo-Christian imagery is just window dressing, but I love it. I think it's hard to imagine a unified belief or willingness or taste from different characters in deciding to go for certain biblical namings. I mean, I can't see much of a reason as to why the namings and whatnot decided by Seele (i.e. 7-eyed mask on Lilin, the name Lilin itself if you want etc), by Gendo (the painting on his office ceiling), Naoko (naming the computer after the Three Wise Men) plus other random things (Asuka's mentioning of Gericho, the pillars of salt in post-SI Antarctica etc) and so on can be pinned down to some sort of common knowledge. Consequently, to me it's a clear case of the authors deliberately creating some sort of fil rouge to hint at a possible spiritual, divine perspective for the whole story. Whereas the more traditional sci-fi reading of NGE would be that of the evolution of a species in the Darwinian sense (humanity vs angels), I like how the spiritual perspective (humans and angels trying to obtain some kind of divine status) is a good intermediate layer betwee the evolutionary one and the psychological one. Let's not forget that this "path to divinity" is basically the main concept behind Japanese shintoism, which in turn is something present in many a manga/anime (pretty much most sport ones). For the record, the Dead Sea Scrolls found in Qumran (I mean, the real ones) are important because, once dated, they truend out to be one of the oldest "copies" of parts of what is considered canon Old Testament for Jewish people.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 23, 2015 3:16 pm SawItAtAge10 wrote: However, in the Evangelion universe, it's a matter of "meaningless" window dressing, and meta-fictive artifacts such as the DSSs being the "real" DSSs that exist in OUR universe...So the naming becomes both literal ad metaphorical at the same time...

[]:             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Tue Jun 23, 2015 3:51 pm NemZ wrote: I just answered this above. They found the alien instructions with the actual dead sea scrolls, with the implication being that primitive desert peoples found the text, misunderstood it, and built real-world religions around these mistakes. These religions explicitly thus do exist in the eva world but are wrong... and the names as used for the angels are in fact correct. You're reading way too much into this. Shinji's focus in the question is on the question of why they're fighting at all, not on who or what they're fighting against.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Jun 23, 2015 4:43 pm What I really want to know is how in the world a bunch of religious zealots in the desert hundreds of years ago managed to decipher something written by aliens. (And how did the SDSS end up in the Middle East at all?)

[]:             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Tue Jun 23, 2015 5:10 pm Reichu wrote: What I really want to know is how in the world a bunch of religious zealots in the desert hundreds of years ago managed to decipher something written by aliens. (And how did the SDSS end up in the Middle East at all?)

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Jun 23, 2015 7:17 pm SawItAtAge10 wrote: It would stand to reason that the two DSSs (from Lilith and Adam) would've been inside their GeoFront caves, i.e. in either Antarctica and Japan...

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 23, 2015 8:02 pm Reichu wrote: What I really want to know is how in the world a bunch of religious zealots in the desert hundreds of years ago managed to decipher something written by aliens. (And how did the SDSS end up in the Middle East at all?)

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 23, 2015 8:41 pm My guess regarding the spears continues to be that the one we know of is in fact both of them working together, hence the spiral shape and two points.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Jun 23, 2015 9:17 pm NemZ wrote: My guess regarding the spears continues to be that the one we know of is in fact both of them working together, hence the spiral shape and two points.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 23, 2015 9:18 pm Yeah, but I don't care about the CI so that's not my problem.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Jun 23, 2015 11:49 pm NemZ wrote: My guess regarding the spears continues to be that the one we know of is in fact both of them working together, hence the spiral shape and two points.

[]:             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Wed Jun 24, 2015 2:15 pm Could it be the weird "Sanskrit" we see on the DSS (which I mistook for beings something closer to stone tablets) is supposed to be Enochian, the traditional "language of the Angels?" This then, of course, falls closer to linguistics and the archaic idea that all languages that exist today stemmed from a single one (which is inherently in correct, since a variety of languages that exist today evolved over time from different sources) and going with the theme, Anno & Co. just ran with it? I mean if the Seeds represent the birth of humanity and human civilization, then who's to say that language as we understand it (within the universe of Evangelion) didn't originate from them as well, and the DSS were ultimately misunderstood over time thus giving rise to false dogmas, i.e. world religions like Christianity/Judaism?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Jun 24, 2015 2:37 pm SawItAtAge10 wrote: Could it be the weird "Sanskrit" we see on the DSS (which I mistook for beings something closer to stone tablets) is supposed to be Enochian, the traditional "language of the Angels?"

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Jun 24, 2015 2:48 pm Burning bush, man. As to the paper: it might look like paper, but it's actually industrial grade teflon with a weathered look!

Now, I can sort of understand the application of the word "God." In that regard, it's a representation of a powerful force/intelligence that simply exists high above and behind the scope of what even our most powerful tools/science can analyze/decipher. However, naming the giant alien creatures that attack after Biblical/Hebrew messengers, doesn't make sense in a country where said religion (which is proven to be a real thing in the Eva-verse via Shinji's conversation with Asuka in ep. 11 about "why they are fighting angels"). So basically, my question is, what is the in-universe explanation for why they name things the way do in regards to bi things like the Seeds/Angels/Magi/Evas? Article: The Naming Conventions in Evangelion
:02 pm All the Judeo-Christian imagery is just window dressing, but I love it. I think it's hard to imagine a unified belief or willingness or taste from different characters in deciding to go for certain biblical namings. I mean, I can't see much of a reason as to why the namings and whatnot decided by Seele (i.e. 7-eyed mask on Lilin, the name Lilin itself if you want etc), by Gendo (the painting on his office ceiling), Naoko (naming the computer after the Three Wise Men) plus other random things (Asuka's mentioning of Gericho, the pillars of salt in post-SI Antarctica etc) and so on can be pinned down to some sort of common knowledge. Consequently, to me it's a clear case of the authors deliberately creating some sort of fil rouge to hint at a possible spiritual, divine perspective for the whole story. Whereas the more traditional sci-fi reading of NGE would be that of the evolution of a species in the Darwinian sense (humanity vs angels), I like how the spiritual perspective (humans and angels trying to obtain some kind of divine status) is a good intermediate layer betwee the evolutionary one and the psychological one. Let's not forget that this "path to divinity" is basically the main concept behind Japanese shintoism, which in turn is something present in many a manga/anime (pretty much most sport ones). For the record, the Dead Sea Scrolls found in Qumran (I mean, the real ones) are important because, once dated, they truend out to be one of the oldest "copies" of parts of what is considered canon Old Testament for Jewish people.

[]:             Quote Post by SawItAtAge10 » Tue Jun 23, 2015 2:54 pm Falcon_of_the_Sun wrote: All the Judeo-Christian imagery is just window dressing, but I love it. I think it's hard to imagine a unified belief or willingness or taste from different characters in deciding to go for certain biblical namings. I mean, I can't see much of a reason as to why the namings and whatnot decided by Seele (i.e. 7-eyed mask on Lilin, the name Lilin itself if you want etc), by Gendo (the painting on his office ceiling), Naoko (naming the computer after the Three Wise Men) plus other random things (Asuka's mentioning of Gericho, the

Consequently, to me it's a clear case of the authors deliberately creating some sort of fil rouge to hint at a possible spiritual, divine perspective for the whole story. Whereas the more traditional sci-fi reading of NGE would be that of the evolution of a species in the Darwinian sense (humanity vs angels), I like how the spiritual perspective (humans and angels trying to obtain some kind of divine status) is a good intermediate layer betwee the evolutionary one and the psychological one. Let's not forget that this "path to divinity" is basically the main concept behind Japanese shintoism, which in turn is something present in many a manga/anime (pretty much most sport ones). For the record, the Dead Sea Scrolls found in Qumran (I mean, the real ones) are important because, once dated, they truend out to be one of the oldest "copies" of parts of what is considered canon Old Testament for Jewish people.
